item.title: Happy Year of the Dragon!
item.description:
Years of the Dragon include 2036, 2024, 2012, 2000, 1988, 1976, 1964, 1952...
People born in Dragon years usually possess natural courage, tenacity, and intelligence, often displaying enthusiasm and confidence.
2024 is the Year of the Wood Dragon, beginning on February 10th, 2024 (Chinese New Year) and concluding on January 28th, 2025 (Chinese New Year's Eve).
The Dragon as a Yang Earth sign possesses resilience and confidence, and are “naturally lucky and successful” due to their intelligence and strong intuition.
In Chinese culture, the Dragon holds a significant place as an auspicious and extraordinary creature, unparalleled in talent and excellence. It symbolizes power, nobility, honor, luck, and success. 2024 is forecasted to bring about opportunities, changes, and challenges. If you're seeking a shift in your current lives, this year might offer a favorable chance.
item.dc:creator: hanmerhack

item.title: A History of DRAGONS in Film & Television, in honor of the Year of the Dragon
item.description:
The Flight of Dragons (1982) - An animated fantasy adventure from Rankin/Bass, based on two seperate books that were combined for the story: "The Flight of Dragons" (1979) a speculative evolution by Peter Dickinson & "The Dragon and the George" (1976) by Gordon R. Dickson https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJFcQiixYFg
Die Nibelungen (1924), a German silent film directed by Fritz Lang. The very first dragon ever depicted in film, based on the ancient Germanic/Norse sagas... in which Siegfried slays the dragon "Fáfnir". https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3i09NJ1AwiM
The Reluctant Dragon (1941). An animated Disney movie based on the children's story by British author Kenneth Grahame. About a dragon who doesn't want to do any of the typical things expected of fairy tale dragons. The original story is seen as a prototype to most modern stories in which the dragon is a sympathetic character rather than a threat. https://i.imgur.com/Oi7SZ2n.mp4
Sleeping Beauty (1959) - The climactic showdown where Maleficent turns into a fearsome dragon. Disney animator Marc Davis designed Maleficent, while animators Wolfgang Reitherman and Eric Cleworth animated her final dragon form - basing her head movements on that of a vicious cobra.
The Wonderful World of the Brothers Grimm (1962) - Directed by George Pal, master of stop motion special effects (who won the Academy Award for his work on "The Time Machine"). The film explored the fictionalized origins of the German brothers who collected and published local fairy tales that became known the world over for centuries to come. This part of the movie, based on "The Singing Bone", told the story of a cowardly knight (Terry Thomas) who sent his squire (Buddy Hackett) to do the dragon slaying for him.
Ghidorah, the Three-Headed Monster (1964) After the success of previous cross-over films such as "King Kong vs. Godzilla" and "Mothra vs. Godzilla", Tomoyuki Tanaka decided to develop a film that would feature Godzilla, Rodan and Mothra. It was also rushed into production after "Red Beard" fell behind schedule and the studio needed a film to fill the New Year's release slot. King Ghidorah was designed as an homage to Yamata no Orochi (a legendary monster in Japanese mythology). It was also decided its origin would be that of an alien from space... as they were tired of the previous tropes where the monsters were all either created by nuclear weapons or were reawakened ancient beasts once worshipped by natives.
Pete's Dragon (1977) - Elliot the dragon was created by Ken Anderson, a key Disney animator who had been with the studio as far back as Snow White (and also animated the eponymous dragon in "The Reluctant Dragon"). Don Bluth did the animation for Elliot. The film is the first involving animation in which none of the Nine Old Men (Disney's original team of animators) were involved. One technique used in the film involved compositing, whereby up to three scenes might be composited together - for example, a live foreground, a live background, and an animated middle ground containing Elliott. Ken Anderson explained that he thought it would be appropriate to make him "a little paunchy" and not always particularly graceful when it comes to flying. Don Hahn, who was assistant director to Don Bluth on the film, gained some experience working with a combination of live-action and animation before later going on to work on "Who Framed Roger Rabbit".
Jabberwocky (1977) - Directed by Terry Gilliam To recreate the illustrated monster of the 19th century storybook, the costume is designed to be worn by a man walking backwards. Hip and knee joints are reversed giving it a bird-like gait. The actor's head is hidden within the monster's torso – the large marionette head on a serpentine neck is controlled by an offscreen pole and lines, which are visible on the print. Long bird-like claws extend out of his shoe heels and his arms become the Jabberwock's wings. Film speed is altered in some scenes to slow the monster's movements and camera angles manipulate perspective in scenes with live actors to depict the monster's immense size.
The Hobbit (1977) - Smaug, voiced by Richard Boone (aka Paladin on "Have Gun - Will Travel"). From the Rankin/Bass animated version of the J.R.R. Tolkien story. The film was animated by Topcraft in Tokyo, a now-defunct Japanese animation studio whose animation team re-formed as Studio Ghibli under Hayao Miyazaki; some of the animators went on to establish Pacific Animation Corporation. According to Rankin, the visual style of the film took its cue from the early illustrations of Arthur Rackham.
Dragonslayer (1981) - VERMITHRAX PERJORATIVE, created by Phil Tippett and Industrial Light & Magic (through a combination of large scale animatronics and stop-motion effects). Guillermo del Toro once stated that along with Maleficent in Sleeping Beauty, Vermithrax is his favorite cinematic dragon. He further stated that: "One of the best and one of the strongest landmarks [of dragon movies] that almost nobody can overcome is Dragonslayer. The design of Vermithrax Pejorative is perhaps one of the most perfect creature designs ever made." Author George R. R. Martin cited the film as a great influence on his series "A Song of Ice and Fire". Ranking it the fifth-best fantasy film of all time, and calling Vermithrax "the best dragon ever put on film [with] the coolest dragon name".
The NeverEnding Story (1984) - Directed by Wolfgang Petersen Falkor, the Luck Dragon was voiced by Alan Oppenheimer (better known to you BOOBS as the voice for Skeletor).
Dragonheart (1996) - Draco, as played by the late Sean Connery. Brought to life by Phil Tippett and Industrial Light and Magic, based on the success they had with the dinosaurs in "Jurassic Park" (and their previous work making dragons in 1981's "Dragonslayer"). https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tiHrU33CzF0
Hercules (1997) - The Hydra was sculpted into a clay model where the dimensions were digitized into the computers as a wire-frame model by which the monster was animated. Early into production, the filmmakers decided the Hydra would ultimately have thirty heads by which the animators created one master head, and the computer could multiply the heads to their desired scale. Overall, thirteen animators and technical directors spent nearly a year-and-a-half creating the four-minute battle sequence.
How to Train Your Dragon (2010) - Toothless the Night Fury Based on the book series by Cressida Cowell, the appearance and personality of Toothless was inspired by cats, dogs, and horses. The face also bears some resemblance to the giant salamander, the largest amphibian in the world. The shape of his face is also reported to have been based on the face of "Stitch" from Lilo & Stitch (2002), also directed by Chris Sanders and Dean DeBlois. Sanders had also previously worked on "The Rescuers Down Under" animating Marahute the giant eagle. You can definitely see the evolution of flying scene from that film "How to Train Your Dragon" made 20 years later: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SbWF-LRlcGw
Game Of Thrones (2011 - 2019) Over the course of the HBO series, based on "A Song of Ice and Fire" by George R.R. Martin, Drogon and his brothers grow from babies into full-sized Valyrian dragons.
The 7th Voyage of Sinbad (1958) - Taro the Dragon battles the Cyclops, brought to life in stop-motion by special effects legend Ray Harryhausen.
Reptilicus (1961) - A rare monster movie from Denmark, with string puppetry for the dragon.
The Munsters (1964) Spot, the family's guard Dragon
Jason and the Argonauts (1963) - The Hydra that protects the Golden Fleece, more of Ray Harryhausen's masterful work.
The Sword in the Stone (1963) - Mad Madam Mim breaks the rules of the "Wizard's Duel" and transforms into a dragon to kill Merlin
Q, The Winged Serpent (1982) - Quetzalcoatl of Aztec mythology is on the rampage in NYC, nesting in the Chrysler Building and picking off innocent victims on the rooftops... in broad daylight I'd think even New Yorkers could spot something like this flying around. The film's special effects for the flying serpent were done using stop-motion animation by Randall William Cook (Ghostbusters, The Lord of the Rings) and David Allen.
Dragon Ball (1984) and Dragon Ball Z (1989) -Shenron
Alice in Wonderland (1985) - The two part TV miniseries of Lewis Carrol's stories ended with a cliffhanger worthy of the phrase "Nightmare Fuel"... the Jabberwock comes for Alice
Knightmare (1987) - Smirkenorff, a dragon that was part of the Fantasy-inspired British gameshow
Willow (1988) - Directed by Ron Howard, the "Eborsisk" was accidently summoned by Willow's magic... and led to one of my favorite scenes in the movies
The Chronicles of Narnia (1989) - Eustace Scrubb, turned into a Dragon after trying to take some cursed treasure. From the BBC's television adapt of the C.S. Lewis series
Eureeka's Castle (1990) - Magellan
Mighty Morphin' Power Rangers (1993) - Dragonzord
Dragonworld (1994) - "Yowler"
The Pagemaster (1994)
Mighty Morphin' Power Rangers (1994) - Thunderzord
Mighty Morphin' Power Rangers (1994) - Serpentera
Orochi, the Eight-Headed Dragon (1994) A loose adaptation of the "Yamata no Orochi" legend (the mythical dragon that inspired the three-headed Ghidorah from the Godzilla series).
Blazing Dragons (1995)
Mulan (1998)
Pokemon (1998) - Charizard vs Magmar
Merlin (1998)
Quest for Camelot (1998) - Eric Idle and Don Rickles as Devon and Cornwall, the two-headed dragon.
Dragon Tales (1999)
Yu-Gi-Oh! (2000) - BLUE EYES WHITE DRAGON
Jackie Chan Adventures (2000) - Shendu
Shrek (2001)
Spirited Away (2001) - Haku, the Dragon spirit of Kohaku River
Reign of Fire (2002) - Dragons are discovered not only to be very real, but they also wiped out the dinosaurs. Only they're not just fossils... they're reawakening after a millenia in hibernation. They quickly overwhlem the modern world with their numbers and destroy it. Now humanity's last few survivors try to eliminate them once and for all.
Avatar: The Last Airbender (2005) - Ran and Sha, the last of the Dragons (and the original Firebending Masters)
Dragons: A Fantasy Made Real (2005) - A ficitonal documentary, narrated by Sir Patrick Stewart, that scientifically explored dragons as if they were real creatures now long extinct
Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire (2005) - The Hungarian Horntail that served as Harry's first challenge in the Tri-Wizard Tournament.
American Dragon: Jake Long (2005)
Eragon (2006) - Saphira the Dragon was voiced by Rachel Weisz in the doomed adapt of the book series by Christopher Paolini.
Beowulf (2007) - Directed by Robert Zemeckis, the dragon was integral to the final part of the original Anglo-Saxon epic... the first piece of English literature to feature a dragonslayer. In the film version, however, the dragon is the bastard son of Beowulf (after being seduced by Grendal's mother).
Enchanted (2008) - Directed by Kevin Lima (A Goofy Movie, The Prince of Egypt, Tarzan). Susan Sarandon played Narissa, created to be similar to three famous Disney Villains - Queen Grimhilde (Snow White), Lady Tremaine (Cinderella) and Maleficent (Sleeping Beauty). Like Maleficent she transforms into a dragon in the finale. Working with the animators, the costume designer incorporated parts of the dragon's form into her character's costume; the cape was designed to look like wings, the layers of the skirt wrap around like a tail and a crown that would turn into horns during Narissa's transformation into a dragon. When filming the scene which sees the transformation of Narissa from a woman into a dragon, a long pole was used to direct the extras' eyelines instead of a laser pointer. Set pieces were made to move back and forth in addition to having a computer-controlled lighting setup and a repeatable head on the camera that were all synchronized. In the film's final sequence, in which Narissa climbs the Woolworth Building while clutching Robert in her claws, a greenscreen rig was built to hold Patrick Dempsey in order to film his face and movements. The rig was a "puppeteering" approach that involved a robotic arm being controlled by three different floor effects artists.
Merlin (2008) - voiced by the late John Hurt
My Little Pony: Friendship is Magic (2010) - Spike the Dragon, I'm sure @ElbowDeepinaHorse will enjoy this inclusion
Tim Burton's Alice in Wonderland (2010) - The Jabberwock, this time voiced by the late Christopher Lee
The Hobbit (2012 & 2013) - Smaug, voiced and motion-captured by Benedict Cumberbatch https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sXN9IHrnVVU
Pete's Dragon (2016)
Kubo and the Two Strings (2016)
The Chronicles of Narnia: Voyage of the Dawn Treader (2010) - This time Eustace Scrubb, cursed by enchanted treasure into becoming a dragon, was played by a young Will Poulter
Kung Fu Panda 3 (2016)
Transformers: The Last Knight (2017) - Dragonstorm
The Dragon Prince (2018) - Azymondias "Zym" reunited with his mother Queen Zubeia, in the fantasy world of Xadia.
Godzilla: King of the Monsters (2019) - King Ghidorah
The Mandalorian (2019) - Krayt Dragon
Raya and the Last Dragon (2021) - Sisu
The Legend of Vox Machina (2022) - The Chroma Conclave
House of the Dragon (2022) - Rhaenyra Targaryen rides Syrax
House of the Dragon (2022) - Prince Daemon Targaryen rides Caraxes, aka the "Blood Wyrm".
House of the Dragon (2022) - Under the banner of the "Greens" they have the dragons Vhagar (the oldest and largest still in existence), Sunfyre and Dreamfyre. Under the banner of the "Blacks" they have the dragons Meleys, Vermax, Arrax, Vermithor, Silverwing and Seasmoke.
Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings (2022) - The Great Protector
Dungeons & Dragons: Honor Among Thieves (2023) - Themberchaud, the Big Chungus... err, I mean, the Red Dragon
Nimona (2023)
Knighty Knight Bugs (1958) "Th-Th-The, Th-Th-The, Th-Th... That's all, folks!"

item.dc:creator: sleeperkid

item.title: LGBTQ members and onlookers chase and beat down armed terrorist.
item.description:
On 25 June 2022, the night before the annual pride parade. two people were killed, and twenty-one wounded in a mass shooting near a popular LGBTQ+ venue in Oslo. The islamic extremist (yellow shirt) was armed with an mp40 submachinegun and a sidearm. When the terrorist suffered a misfire, a patron tackled him, which made him drop his main firearm. This was followed by other people pursuing the terrorist, and ended with him pinned down on the side of the street, as can be seen in the video. The police formally cancelled the pride parade due to increased threat levels.
Despite this, hundreds of people marched in Oslo, chanting: "We're here, we're queer, we won't disappear!" the morning after. Bjarne Finsrud, the patron who first attacked the terrorist, is scheduled to receive the Norwegian medal of noble deed in gold.

item.title: It's ok to laugh. It's weird, I think sometimes we forget it's ok to be happy while we're struggling.
item.description: So, like, laugh and stuff. Or at stuff. But not at folks, unless they're, ya know what? Just live your lives. https://www.gofundme.com/f/work-and-personal-clothes-and-shoes-etc?utm_source=customer&utm_medium=copy_link&utm_campaign=p_lico+share-sheet Sorry :( you may see a shameless plug, but trust me. There's plenty of shame there. Feel free to ignore it, just thought I'd leave it there.

item.title: Dogs are the best people.
item.description: This is Dodger. He's a guide dog for his human Roland Croteau, a blind senior in British Columbia. The duo found themselves stranded after one cab driver refused to pick them up, citing an allergy, and a second one ultimately dropped them off at the wrong house. Fortunately, Dodger was ready to step up, guiding Roland safely back home. The cab company, Vernon Taxi, has offered free rides to Croteau and said it'll use this unfortunate opportunity to retrain its staff.
item.dc:creator: Rammsteinstochter
